description: THESAUROS content-storage known-answer tests (design: docs/design-storage.md, spec/platform.md §7). THESAUROS is the platform's O-Foundation content-storage organ over the L4 erasure store. These vectors pin the language-agnostic content-addressing wire so any implementation reproduces content ids (CIDs) and proof-of-retrievability responses byte-for-byte. An object is split into LEAF-sized leaves; each leaf is hashed WITH ITS INDEX (position-bound) and a binary Merkle tree over those leaf hashes gives the CID = the root — the content address and the storage commitment at once. All hashes are BLAKE3 via hash_labeled (BLAKE3 keyed by the 32-byte BLAKE3 hash of the ASCII label). Pinned by the Rust reference suite (fanos-thesauros/tests/conformance.rs). Byte strings are lower-case hex; integers decimal.

params.root: binary Merkle fold of the leaf hashes; a lone odd node at any level is promoted unchanged; empty object = one empty leaf, cid = leaf_hash(0, [])

single_leaf.comment: An object smaller than one leaf is a single leaf; its CID is that leaf's position-bound hash.

two_leaf.comment: 0xAB repeated 4096 (one full leaf) then 0xCD x100 (a partial second leaf). CID = node_hash(leaf_hash(0, leaf0), leaf_hash(1, leaf1)). The proof-of-retrievability response for leaf index 1 is the 100 leaf bytes plus a 1-step Merkle path whose sibling is leaf 0's hash (on the left).

manifest.comment: An object of CHUNK*2 + 500 bytes, byte i = (i*7) mod 256, split into 3 chunks (2 full + 1 partial). The manifest encodes count(4, LE) || [ cid(32) || len(4, LE) ] x count; its own CID is chunk_cid(manifest.encode()).
